revision.text:
[[ja:別宮大山祇神社]] {{Infobox religious building | Name = Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine (別宮大山祇神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Bekku Oyamazumi jinja 03.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Imabari, Ehime|en|Imabari, Ehime|qid=Q461020|lt=Imabari, Ehime|1=Imabari, Ehime}}, Ehime Prefecture | Address = 3-6-1 Bekku-chō, Imabari, Ehime Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|4|7.4|N|132|59|42.4|E|region:JP-38_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine|qq=}} | Deities = [[Ōyamatsumi]] (大山積大神) | type = {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} Bekku
Former '''Prefectural Shrine''' (県社) | Founded = 703 CE (Taihō 3) | Alternative Names = Mishima Ji-Gozen (三島地御前) | Pilgrimage Route = {{ill|Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501519}}, Site 40 | Main Festival = [[May 10]] | Map = {{location map|Japan Ehime|lat_deg=34|lat_min=4|lat_sec=7.4|lon_deg=132|lon_min=59|lon_sec=42.4|label=Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine|position=right|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|qq=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|34|4|7.4|N|132|59|42.4|E|qq=}}|qq=}} }} '''Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine''' (別宮大山祇神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in '''Imabari, Ehime Prefecture'''. It is a '''Bekku (別宮, "secondary shrine")''' of {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}, the '''Ichinomiya (highest-Ranked shrine) of Iyo Province'''. It was formerly classified as a '''Prefectural Shrine (県社)''' and serves as '''Site 40 of the Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage (新四国曼荼羅霊場).''' == Overview == [[File:別宮大山祇神社 鳥居.JPG|200px|thumb|Torii Gate]] Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine is situated in central '''Imabari''', facing '''National Route 317'''. It is the Bekku (secondary shrine) of the '''Great Shrine of Japan’s Protector deity (日本総鎮守)''', {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}, located on '''Ōmishima Island in the Seto Inland Sea'''. In the past, it was said to rival its main shrine in grandeur.Ehime Prefecture Shrine Office The '''worship hall, built during the Sengoku period''', has been designated as an '''Ehime Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property'''. Following the '''Tenshō War Fires''' (天正の兵火), Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine was managed by '''Nankōbō (南光坊)''', a temple that acted as its '''Bettō-ji (shrine-affiliated temple)'''. During the Edo period, pilgrimage records referred to it as: *"Japan’s Grand Protector deity (日本総鎮守) Ōyamazumi Daimyōjin, Bettō Nankōbō."*1825 pilgrimage record After the '''Meiji Restoration’s separation of Shinto and Buddhism (神仏分離)''', Nankōbō was formally separated, and the shrine’s '''pilgrimage site number was reassigned'''. However, even today, visiting both Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine and '''Nankōbō''' remains customary. == Enshrined deity == - '''Ōyamatsumi no Ōkami (大山積大神)''' – The deity of '''mountains and warriors''' - The shrine is an '''offshoot of Ōmishima’s Ōyamazumi Shrine''', but its '''name and deity’s representation differ from the main shrine.''' == History == [[File:Bekku20210511 1.jpg|thumb|Worship Hall and Main Hall]] According to shrine records, in '''703 CE (Taihō 3),''' '''Ochi Tamazumi''' (越智玉澄), younger brother of '''Ochi Tamaoki''' (伊予国守), enshrined the deity here following an '''imperial decree from Emperor Monmu'''. The shrine was designated as a '''Bekku (secondary shrine)''' and became known as "Ji-Gozen" (地御前). In '''712 CE (Wadō 5),''' the shrine hall was completed (some sources consider this the official founding date). - The '''main shrine of Ōyamazumi''' was begun in '''701 CE (Taihō 1)''' but only completed and consecrated in '''719 CE (Yōrō 3)'''. - This suggests that Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine '''predates the finalization of the main shrine’s construction.''' - The shrine’s priesthood was managed by the '''Bekku family''', an offshoot of the '''Ōhori family (main shrine priests).''' During the '''Shōji era (1199–1200 CE),''' eight '''monk lodgings (僧坊)''' from '''Ōmishima''' were relocated here, establishing '''Bettō-ji Kōmyō-ji (大積山光明寺)''' as the shrine’s overseeing temple. During '''Kōnin (810–824 CE),''' '''Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi)''' visited the shrine, performing '''Buddhist rituals''' at its monastic quarters. This later influenced its inclusion in the '''Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage''', with '''Kōmyō-ji''' handling '''pilgrim registrations'''. A full '''pilgrimage''' included visiting the '''main shrine on Ōmishima''' as well, where pilgrims would perform '''additional prayers at Jingū-ji (神宮寺).''' '''Edo-period pilgrimage records''' list it as: *"Japan’s Grand Protector deity, Mishima Main Shrine, Bettō Jingū-ji."*1856 pilgrimage record In '''1551 CE (Tenmon 20),''' the shrine was '''struck by lightning and burned down'''. It was later '''rebuilt in 1575 CE (Tenshō 3) by Kurushima Michisada''' (来島通総). During the '''Tenshō Period (1573–1592 CE),''' the '''Chōsokabe clan’s army burned down''' the eight monk lodgings. Only '''Nankōbō''' was later rebuilt, becoming the shrine’s sole Bettō-ji. During the '''Edo period''', the shrine was patronized by '''Tōdō Takatora''' and successive '''Imabari Domain lords'''. After the '''Meiji Restoration''', the shrine’s '''Buddhist images and pilgrimage role were transferred to Nankōbō''', and its name was standardized to '''Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine (別宮大山祇神社).''' == Cultural Properties == === Ehime Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Worship Hall (拝殿)''' – '''Built in 1575 CE,''' designated in '''1965 CE'''. - '''Rikai-ni’s Stone Lantern (理海尼の石灯籠)''' – Designated '''1984 CE'''. - '''Ema "White Horse" (絵馬「白馬の図」)''' – Designated '''1965 CE'''. === Imabari City Natural Monuments === - '''Camphor Tree (クスノキ)''' – Designated '''1974 CE'''. == Subordinate Shrines (Sessha and Massha) == - '''Anaba Shrine (阿奈波神社)''' – Dedicated to '''Iwanaga-hime''', daughter of Ōyamatsumi. - '''Narabara Shrine (奈良原神社)''' – Enshrines a spirit relocated from '''Narabara Shrine in former Ochi District'''. - '''Kiyotaka Inari Shrine (清高稲荷神社)''' - '''Kōjin Shrine (荒神社)''' – Dedicated to '''Sanbō Kōjin''' and '''Ōkuninushi'''. File:阿奈波神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Anaba Shrine File:奈良原神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Narabara Shrine File:Bekku Oyamazumi jinja 06.jpg|Kiyotaka Inari Shrine File:荒神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Kōjin Shrine == Access == - '''Rail:''' '''8-minute walk from JR Yosan Line Imabari Station''' - '''Nearby Site:''' '''Nankōbō (Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage Site 55)''' == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} (Main Shrine) == External Links == * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=792 Bekku Ōyamazumi Shrine (Ehime Prefecture Shrine Office)] * {{translated page|ja|別宮大山祇神社|version=103595828|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:別宮大山祇神社]] [[ja:大山祇神社 (今治市大三島)]] [[ja:大山祇神社 (西の谷)]] [[ja:大山積神社 (新居浜市)]] [[ja:大山祇神社 (新上五島町道土井郷)]] [[ja:大山祇神社 (新上五島町今里郷)]] [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = 別宮大山祇神社 |image = [[File:Bekku Oyamazumi jinja 03.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}{{ill|Imabari, Ehime|en|Imabari, Ehime|qid=Q461020|lt=Imabari City|1=Imabari, Ehime}}Bekku-cho 3-chome 6-1 |Coordinates = {{Wiki coordinates 2-tier DMS|34|4|7.4|N|132|59|42.4|E|region:JP-38_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=別宮大山祇神社|qq=}} |Deity = {{ill|Ooyamazumi-no-Okami|ja|大山祇神|lt=Ooyamazumi-no-Okami|lt_ja=大山積大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ooyamazumi-no-Okami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q386563}} |Rank = {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ooyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}別宮
Old {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |Founding = {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|qid=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}}3 year ({{ill|703|en|703|qid=Q23500|lt=703 AD|1=703}}) |Main Hall = |Also known as = Mishima Jigozen |Pilgrimage number = {{ill|Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501519}} No. 40 |Annual Festival = {{ill|May 10|en|May 10|qid=Q2557|lt=May 10|1=May 10}} |Sacred Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ehime|lat_deg=34|lat_min=4|lat_sec=7.4|lon_deg=132|lon_min=59|lon_sec=42.4|label=別宮大山祇神社|position=right|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|qq=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|34|4|7.4|N|132|59|42.4|E|qq=}}|qq=}} }} '''Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja''' (べっくおおやまづみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}{{ill|Imabari, Ehime|en|Imabari, Ehime|qid=Q461020|lt=Imabari City|1=Imabari, Ehime}}. It is the branch shrine of the main shrine of {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|qid=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}}’s {{ill|Ichinomiya|en|Ichinomiya|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ichinomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ooyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}, and under the modern shrine Ranking system its former Rank was that of a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. It is designated as No. 40 on the {{ill|Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501519}}. == Overview == [[File:別宮大山祇神社 鳥居.JPG|200px|thumb|Torii]] Situated in the center of {{ill|Imabari, Ehime|en|Imabari, Ehime|qid=Q461020|lt=Imabari City|1=Imabari, Ehime}}, facing {{ill|Japan National Route 317|en|Japan National Route 317|qid=Q6157219|lt=Route 317|1=Japan National Route 317}}, it is the branch shrine of Japan’s general guardian shrine, the main shrine enshrined on the [[Seto Inland Sea|{{ill|Seto Inland Sea|en|Seto Inland Sea|qid=Q231312|lt=Seto Inland Sea|1=Seto Inland Sea}}]]’s {{ill|Ōmishima Island|en|Ōmishima Island|qid=Q9009869|lt=Dai-Setsushima|1=Ōmishima Island}}. It is said that in ancient times it boasted a grandeur comparable to that of the main shrine{{ill|Ehime Prefecture Shrine Office page|ja|愛媛県神社庁ページより|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehime Prefecture Shrine Office page|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The worship hall constructed during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117415390}} has been designated as a tangible cultural property of Ehime Prefecture. == Deity == * '''{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|qid=Q386563|lt=Ooyamazumi-no-Okami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}''' (Ooyamazumi-no-Okami) * Invited from {{ill|Ōmishima Island|en|Ōmishima Island|qid=Q9009869|lt=Dai-Setsushima|1=Ōmishima Island}} via {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}. As with the main shrine, the inscriptions for the shrine name and deity differ. == History == [[File:Bekku20210511 1.jpg|thumb|Worship Hall/Main Hall]] According to the shrine legend, in Taihō 3 (703 AD), Ochi Tamatsumi{{ill|Iyo Province Guardian – Younger Brother of Ochi Tamakou|ja|伊予国守・越智玉興の弟。|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo Province Guardian – Younger Brother of Ochi Tamakou|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was ordered by Emperor Monmu to invite the deity from {{ill|Ōmishima Island|en|Ōmishima Island|qid=Q9009869|lt=Dai-Setsushima|1=Ōmishima Island}} to {{ill|Hiyoshigō, Ochi District|ja|越智郡日吉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshigō, Ochi District|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, establishing it as the branch (Jigozen) of the main shrine, Ooyamazumi Shrine. In Wadō 5 (712 AD), the shrine building was completed (some theories consider this as the founding date). The main shrine was initiated in Taihō 1 (701 AD) and its building was completed with the relocation ceremony held in Yōrō 3 (719 AD), so it may be said that the branch shrine is older. Thereafter, a branch family of the main shrine’s priestly lineage (the Shakeya and Daishukeya) served as the priests here. During the Shōji era (1199–1200), eight of the twenty-four monk quarters of the main shrine were relocated from Dai-Setsushima to the branch shrine and designated as subsidiary chapels of the separate temple, Daisekizan Kōmyō-ji. Furthermore, during the Kōnin era (810–824), when Kūkai visited and conducted religious rites in the quarters, it later became designated as one of the 88 pilgrimage sites of Shikoku, with the offering ritual performed at the separate temple. Since the formal pilgrimage involves crossing the sea to visit the main shrine on Dai-Setsushima and performing the ritual at the separate temple Jingu-ji, the Edo period pilgrimage record states “Japan’s General Guardian Shrine, Mishima Main Shrine, Separate Temple Jingu-ji”{{ill|1856 Pilgrimage Record|ja|1856th year の納経帳による。|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1856 Pilgrimage Record|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and being an additional visit, no site number was assigned. In Tenbun 20 (1551 AD), the shrine building was consumed by fire due to a lightning strike, and it was rebuilt in Tenshō 3 (1575 AD) by Raishima Dōsō. The eight monk quarters were burned down during the Tenshō era (1573–1592) by the forces of the Chōsōgū clan, and later only the Nankōbō was reconstructed, becoming the separate temple of this shrine where the offering ritual came to be performed. In the early modern period, it was protected by successive lords of the {{ill|Imabari Domain|ja|今治藩主|lt=Imabari Domain|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imabari Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} including Tōdō Takakage. In the early Meiji period, due to the separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the honji (Buddhist deities) enshrined in the main hall – the Great Tachi Chishō Nyorai, the two attendant deities, the Sixteen Princes, and the pilgrimage designation – were transferred to the Nankōbō, thereby dissolving the separate temple relationship. Additionally, as with the main shrine, the shrine name was changed from Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja (using 大山積神社) to Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja (using 大山祇神社). In Showa 12, a new shrine building was constructed. At that time, the worship hall was relocated elsewhere and repurposed as an Ema hall. In Showa 20 (1945), although most of Imabari City was destroyed by U.S. air raids—with only the main hall remaining—the former worship hall (which had been relocated and used as an Ema hall) survived. During reconstruction, the former worship hall was returned to its original location and once again served as the worship hall. == Shrine Grounds == [[File:別宮大山祇神社 本殿.JPG|200px|thumb|Main Hall]] * Main Hall * Worship Hall – Rebuilt in Tenshō 3 (1575 AD) by Raishima Dōsō. It features a 5-bay column span, 4-bay beam span, single-storied, gabled structure with cypress bark roofing. In Kyōhō 4 (1719 AD), all the front worship areas were expanded. In Showa 37 (1962 AD), dismantling, repair, and restoration work were carried out.[https://ehime-c.esnet.ed.jp/bunkazai/kennobunkazai/shiteibunkazai/kenzoubutsu/pdf-files/ken/1-1-14bekkuooyamazumijinjahaiden.pdf Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja Worship Hall] (Ehime Prefecture Cultural Property Protection Division). Designated as a tangible cultural property by Ehime Prefecture. * Torii – The lintel bears the inscription “Japan’s General Guardian Shrine, Mishima Jigozen.” == Subsidiary Shrines == * {{ill|Anaba Shrine|ja|阿奈波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503429|1=Anaba Shrine}} (Anabajinja) – The enshrined deity is {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|en|Iwanaga-hime|qid=Q8191705|lt=Iwanagahime|1=Iwanaga-hime}} (the eldest daughter of Ooyamazumi-no-Okami), and it is a branch of the Anaba Shrine on Dai-Setsushima. * Narahara Shrine – The enshrined deity is a branch spirit of Narahara Shrine from the summit of Narahara Mountain (formerly Tamagawa-cho, Ochi District); when all the parishioners of Narahara Shrine relocated to Imabari City, the branch spirit was enshrined within these grounds. * Kiyotaka Inari Shrine * Aragami Shrine – The enshrined deities are {{ill|Sanpō Aragami|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanpō Aragami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Sanbo Kojin|qid=Q13577821}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}. The shrine building was constructed in Kansei 6 (1794) as a Marishi-ten shrine. File:阿奈波神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Anaba Shrine File:奈良原神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Narahara Shrine File:Bekku Oyamazumi jinja 06.jpg|Kiyotaka Inari Shrine File:荒神社 (別宮大山祇神社境内社).JPG|Aragami Shrine == Major Festivals == * New Year's Festival ({{ill|January 1|en|January 1|qid=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}}) * Ininen Festival ({{ill|February 17|en|February 17|qid=Q2341|lt=February 17|1=February 17}}) * Hatsu-uma Festival (old date: First Horse Day in February) * Annual Festival (Second Saturday) * Wagoe (Natsu-goshi) Festival ({{ill|July 29|en|July 29|qid=Q2717|lt=July 29|1=July 29}}) * Niiname-sai (Harvest Festival) ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|qid=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}}) * New Year's Eve Festival ({{ill|December 31|en|December 31|qid=Q2912|lt=December 31|1=December 31}}) == Cultural Properties == === Ehime Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Worship Hall – Designated on March 29, Showa 40. === Imabari City Designated Cultural Properties === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Rikainei Stone Lantern – Designated on April 14, Showa 59. ** Ema “Picture of a White Horse” – Designated on December 22, Showa 40. * Natural Monuments ** {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|qid=Q158722|lt=Camphor Tree|1=Camphora officinarum}} – Designated on July 6, Showa 49. == Adjacent Pilgrimage Sites == ; {{ill|Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|ja|新四国曼荼羅霊場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Sites|1=Shin-Shikoku Mandara Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501519}} : 39 {{ill|Chikurin-ji|ja|竹林寺 (今治市)|lt=Chikurin-ji, Imabari|lt_ja=竹林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikurin-ji|1=Chikurin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q104762114}} --- '''40 Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja''' --- 41 {{ill|Kourin-ji|ja|光林寺 (今治市)|lt=Kourin-ji, Imabari|lt_ja=光林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kourin-ji|1=Kourin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17991048}} == Local Information == '''Location''' * {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}{{ill|Imabari, Ehime|en|Imabari, Ehime|qid=Q461020|lt=Imabari City|1=Imabari, Ehime}}Bekku-cho 3-chome 6-1 '''Transportation Access''' * Railway: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|qid=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku|1=Shikoku Railway Company}} ({{ill|Yosan Line|en|Yosan Line|qid=Q575016|lt=Yosan Line|1=Yosan Line}}) {{ill|Imabari Station|en|Imabari Station|qid=Q875869|lt=Imabari Station|1=Imabari Station}} (approximately 8 minutes on foot) '''Surroundings''' * {{ill|Nankōbō|ja|南光坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nankōbō|1=Nankou-bou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407501}} – No. 55 on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Pilgrimage Sites|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. == Footnotes == {{Footnote Help|qq=}} {{reflist|qq=}} == References == * 『Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Ehime Prefecture』 ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|qid=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}) – Imabari City, entry on Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja. * 『Dai-Setsushima Pilgrimage』 (Published by Ooyamazumi Shrine Office) – entries on Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja and Nankōbō. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ooyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} (in Imabari City, Dai-Setsushima Town) – the main shrine. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Bekku Ooyamazumi-jinja|qq=}} *
